model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"With only 256 optimizer steps on a flat CIFAR-10 MLP, the baseline is likely data-limited and undertrained. Increasing batch size from 64 to 256 lets the model cover roughly a full epoch within the fixed-step budget, while a modest LR increase compensates for the larger batch. This is a simple, low-risk first move that directly targets budget efficiency without changing architecture or training logic.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The optimization-side changes are already paying off, so the next clean lever is modestly increasing MLP capacity. Raising `BASE_CHANNELS` from 12 to 16 expands the hidden widths from 192/384 to 256/512 with no control-flow complexity, giving the now-better-tuned optimizer more representational headroom within the same 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The current script is already using a larger batch, a step-aware scheduler, and a somewhat larger MLP, so the next low-complexity lever is decoupled weight decay. Switching from Adam to AdamW often improves generalization for short-budget training without disturbing the rest of the optimization setup, making it a clean follow-up after the successful layout and schedule changes.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The current training setup is now stronger on batch size, schedule, model width, and optimizer choice, and the AdamW swap produced only a marginal gain. That points to a near-neighbor scalar retune: a modest LR increase is a simple way to push faster progress inside the same 256-step budget without adding complexity or changing the training dynamics qualitatively.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The recent LR increase still improved loss, which suggests the current optimization setup can support a somewhat stronger model. Increasing depth from 2 to 3 is a simple capacity extension for this MLP and should give the already-successful batch/schedule/LR configuration more nonlinear modeling power without changing the overall training structure.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The deeper 3-layer MLP improved substantially under the current schedule, which suggests optimization headroom remains. A modest LR increase to `2e-3` is the simplest next probe to see whether the larger model can use the fixed 256-step budget more aggressively without changing the rest of the successful setup.","secondary_modes":["layout","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The move from `1.5e-3` to `2e-3` regressed validation loss, so the simplest high-confidence next step is to roll back to the last better scalar setting before exploring other directions. This preserves the gains from the deeper architecture while undoing the one recent change that hurt performance.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 3e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The current configuration appears close to a good optimization regime, so a small regularization retune is a sensible next probe. With AdamW, increasing `WEIGHT_DECAY` modestly can improve generalization for the now-deeper and wider MLP without changing the successful batch size, scheduler, or learning-rate shape.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strong earlier gain from increasing batch size suggests this workload benefits from seeing more data within the fixed 256-step budget. Moving from `256` to `512` is a simple continuation of that idea: it increases example throughput and effective epoch coverage without changing the model or optimizer, making it a clean next experiment now that other knobs appear near-tuned.","secondary_modes":["topk","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The jump to `BATCH_SIZE = 512` produced a large gain, which suggests the optimizer can likely use a slightly larger step size at this new batch scale. Retesting `2e-3` is more justified here than it was at batch 256, because the larger batch should support a more aggressive learning rate while preserving the rest of the now-strong configuration.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"At `BATCH_SIZE = 512`, raising the learning rate to `2e-3` regressed validation loss, so the clean next step is to revert to the last better scalar setting. {"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The move from batch 256 to 512 delivered the largest gain in the run, indicating this fixed-step workload is heavily throughput-limited. Testing `BATCH_SIZE = 1024` is the most direct continuation of that signal: it further increases data coverage per optimizer step while leaving the now-strong optimizer, schedule, and architecture untouched.","secondary_modes":["topk","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The move to `BATCH_SIZE = 1024` produced another large jump, which changes the effective optimization regime enough to justify revisiting the learning-rate scale. A moderate increase to `2e-3` is the cleanest next test: with the larger batch, the model may benefit from a more aggressive step size while keeping the same architecture and schedule.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-3","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 3e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"With the large-batch regime now doing most of the work, the next sensible lever is regularization strength rather than more model capacity or even larger batches. A moderate increase in AdamW weight decay to `1e-3` is a simple test of whether the current high-throughput setup can generalize a bit better without disturbing the successful schedule and learning-rate scale.","secondary_modes":["indexing","micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 2048","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strongest gains in this run have come from increasing batch size under the fixed 256-step budget, and even `1024` remains stable with the current optimizer and schedule. With only a few trials left, the highest-upside follow-up is to continue that successful direction and test `BATCH_SIZE = 2048`, which maximizes data coverage per step without adding code complexity.","secondary_modes":["topk","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The latest large improvement came from moving to `BATCH_SIZE = 2048`, which materially changes the stable optimization scale. {"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The last learning-rate increase slightly regressed from the strong `BATCH_SIZE = 2048` configuration, so the highest-confidence final move is to roll back to `2e-3`.
